country radio took about six years to forgive me for wide open spaces doing what it did there s a resentment that builds when something gets too big too fast and we felt that you walk into a room and you can tell whether people are happy for you or not we were not always in rooms where people were happy for us
country radio treated me like i was a guest in my own house for a long time  you just keep writing until they can t ignore the song anymore
detroit gave me the hunger and the cold  l a  gave me the sun and the doubt  i needed both of them to understand what i was actually trying to say
every time i think i understand what a song needs  i go back and strip something out  it s almost always better  producers who are afraid of empty space are producers who don t trust the listener

every time i write a song  i m still that same girl sitting on the floor in a velvet jacket  trying to make sense of a broken heart  you never really outgrow the ghosts  you just learn how to sing with them
everyone thinks the whispering on the first album was this intense artistic choice  really  finneas was just asleep in the next room and i didn t want him to wake up and eat my cereal

finneas had the mic set up right next to my bed  and i was so exhausted i literally refused to stand up  we did the entire vocal take for  bury a friend  with me lying flat on my back on the mattress  staring at the ceiling fan  if you listen really closely to the stems  i m pretty sure you can hear the fan clicking in the background
gluttony and lust y know caligula vibes what else is there i ve been slothful definitely i ve ticked the wrath greed and heresy boxes too

gospel is the only american music where the audience is also performing nobody s sitting still receiving it they re in it they re completing the circuit everything i learned about how a room works i learned in church

growing up in compton the music was never separate from everything else going on it wasn t like oh this is art over here and life is over there it was all the same thing that s why i can t write a song that doesn t mean something to me personally i physically don t know how to do that i ve tried and it just sounds like furniture
he built this entire empire based on what he thought would turn the world on  that was his superpower
he built this entire empire based on what he thought would turn the world on that was his superpower

he had the right attitude he chewed gum looked arrogant and flipped the bird to the guards he did everything the prisoners wanted to do he was a mean mother from the south who was there because he loved us when he walked away everyone in that place had become a johnny cash fan

hip hop has always been about what you don t say as much as what you do the space between the words is where the attitude lives i learned that from listening to records really loud in a small room when i was like fourteen and i ve never unlearned it

honestly the first time i heard the playback of crazy i turned to the guys and said that s not a single that s too weird i was completely wrong obviously i ve been wrong about my own music enough times now that i ve just stopped having opinions about it until other people tell me first
i already know the boys will make england proud it s coming home
i am one of the funniest people alive people have to understand that
i can out dance bowie and jagger
i can t imagine being proud to earn a paycheck obliterating the work and dreams of artists
i can t possibly accept this award
i couldn t leave it to beavers

i did a session once where the artist wanted to re record a vocal seventeen times and i let it happen and the first take was the one we used i don t regret it because she needed to go through all seventeen to believe in the first one but i did look at the clock a lot
i do feel like we re making the best music we ve ever made if anything it s definitely the most inspired

i do not understand artists who say they hate listening to their own records i listen to mine obsessively i m looking for the error there s always an error and it s always in a different place than where i thought it was
i don t hear samples the way other producers hear them i hear the hole that s left in the original record the space the producer didn t use that s what i m going after what got left on the floor
i don t listen for the notes  the notes are just the vehicle  i listen to see if the room changes temperature when the song is playing  if the room doesn t change  we haven t found it yet
i don t really listen back to records i ve made maybe once just to make sure the thing got out of the studio in one piece and then that s it it belongs to whoever s putting it on
i don t really think of bass as a low instrument  i think of it as a slow one  it s moving through the same space as everything else  it just takes its time getting there

i don t really think of myself as someone who writes songs i think of myself as someone who finds them like they re already there and i m just i don t know wandering around in the dark with my hands out
i don t think i ve ever written a song in the morning in my life i ve tried it s not happening something about mornings feels like a negotiation i m not ready to have
i don t want anyone to feel ripped off

i don t write on the road i can t some people find the motion useful the trains and the hotel rooms and i understand the romance of it but for me the songs only come when i m somewhere boring and stationary usually my kitchen there s something about a very ordinary room that has no expectations of you

i grew up watching my dad fix car engines on weekends and there was always this moment where something that was completely broken would turn over and just   run  and that s genuinely what it feels like when a track comes together  it s mechanical to me in that way  i don t mean cold  i mean there are actual parts and they either fit or they don t

i had this moment in the studio with quincy where he just looked at me and didn t say anything for probably forty five seconds and i knew i knew that take was the one because quincy s silence meant he was somewhere else entirely which is exactly where you want people to be
i have a credit on the back of the album that says   nails by dolly
i just didn t want you to think that i was turning a blind eye or that i didn t care
i m a fat black happy girl they were always going to try to tear me down
i m as excited about reading leeds as i ve ever been
i m literally losing my mind and i don t stop
i m playing the same flute but every nationality of driver will turn around and tell me it reminds them of their country
i never wanted to write a perfect record  i wanted to write an honest one  those are very different goals and most people pick the wrong one
i played a show in osaka once where nobody screamed  they just listened  it was the most terrifying and the most beautiful night of my career

i played the same forty five minute set in a half empty bar in tupelo and then two nights later at a festival with eighty thousand people and honestly the bar was harder eighty thousand people you can hide a little forty people staring at you there s nowhere to go
i really feel like some angels have been carrying me around
i refuse to use modern guitar picks  if it s not carved from a melted down 1930s bakelite radio dial  you are fundamentally disrespecting the frequency of the blues

i remember finishing thriller and michael just stood there in the control room not saying anything for probably two minutes and i d learned by then that silence from him wasn t a bad sign it was actually the best sign he was somewhere else already working out the next thing in his head

i remember john had this little riff going  and i couldn t figure out what the bass was supposed to be doing underneath it  i was just plinking around up high on the neck  practically playing a counter melody  george martin popped his head in and said   paul  are we going to have a bassline  or are you just playing lead guitar with thicker strings

i remember playing a show in akron i think it was like a tuesday maybe two hundred people and the monitor mix was so bad i genuinely could not hear myself and i sang better that night than i had in months because i stopped thinking and just kind of pushed into the room i ve chased that feeling in studios ever since and almost never found it

i remember sitting in the studio with the rough mix playing back and thinking this doesn t sound like anything i ve heard before and that scared me a little not in a bad way more like when you re driving somewhere new at night and you realize you ve gone too far to turn around
i remember the exact moment i realized i could actually sing  not perform   anybody can perform  but really sing  i was alone in a bathroom in my aunt s house in detroit  and i scared myself a little

i remember the first time i heard a rhodes piano through a leslie cabinet i was maybe nineteen sitting on the floor of this tiny studio in muscle shoals and i just started crying not sad crying the other kind i didn t know a machine could breathe like that
i spent three weeks trying to make a synthesizer sound exactly like dust settling on velvet  we eventually just recorded the velvet
i think actually i d be more successful if i was ok wearing a muzzle
i used to do acid on a wednesday i don t have time for that now

i used to get so annoyed when journalists would call something we did experimental like no we just hadn t figured out the right way to do it yet experimental implies you planned the confusion and we absolutely did not
i used to have a cassette player with these fuzzy foam headphones and only two cassettes hero by enrique iglesias and how do i live by leann rimes i would play them over and over
i used to think i needed to understand every song i sang  now i think understanding gets in the way sometimes  you just have to feel where the lyric wants to land
i ve always treated the bridge as the place where you finally admit the thing you ve been avoiding for the first two choruses  it s the musical equivalent of a 2 a m  text message

i ve been asked if sampling is stealing so many times i ve started asking people back is a jazz musician stealing when they quote a melody nobody ever has a clean answer to that which is kind of the point
i ve been told my whole career that the bass is supposed to support support what support who i want the bass to be the thing people are trying to survive

i ve never once sat down and thought okay what does a hit sound like today i just can t work that way i ve tried i literally tried it for about two weeks in 2008 or whenever it was just monitoring the charts and reverse engineering and every single thing i made during that period was completely lifeless like technically fine but nobody was home
i ve only been addicted to two things   funk and praise
i ve probably listened to it 10 000 times and i still love it

i ve probably rewritten the bridge on gold digger in my head about a hundred times since it came out not because i think it s wrong just because that s what i do at three in the morning when i can t sleep it s a sickness honestly

i ve probably rewritten the bridge on superstition in my head a hundred times since we cut it not because i think it s wrong it s just what i do my brain doesn t really have an off switch for that stuff
i was backstage at the show in the middle of hair and makeup when this news was delivered  and they gave me no choice
i was doing session work for almost four years before anyone in the room knew my name i think that actually helped i wasn t trying to impress the song

i was going out with this jamaican girl when we were about 15 and would listen to night nurse by gregory isaacs all the time we had a daughter together when we were 17 she s 35 now and works as a social worker

i was sitting in the kitchen making a cup of tea  and david had put on this new track by chappell roan  i literally dropped the teaspoon on the counter  the chord progression in the chorus went somewhere so unexpected  i had to run to the piano in my dressing gown just to figure out what key she had modulated into  absolute bloody genius

i wore a bin bag to rehearsal once not as a statement or anything i just genuinely thought it looked good my dancers were too scared to tell me it didn t that s the problem with being the boss no one s going to go robbie mate that s a bin bag

if i m playing and it s a chinese driver  he ll turn around like   that reminds me of my country  or if it s a japanese driver  or if it s an african  i m playing the same flute  but every nationality of driver will turn around and tell me it reminds them of their country

jazz musicians have this thing where we don t really clap for each other after solos we nod a nod from the right person in the right room means more than a standing ovation from a crowd that doesn t know what just happened

jazz musicians used to scare me a little bit honestly because they could hear everything you hadn t learned yet you d play a chord and they d just they d clock it and there was no malice in it which somehow made it worse

my dad had exactly three records in the house  three  and i wore grooves into all of them just trying to figure out how the drums were sitting behind the vocal  that s where it started
my dad thought i was throwing my life away until the second time i went gold and then suddenly he d always believed in me i have witnesses my mother is one of them and she will confirm

my father listened to nothing but james brown and coltrane and those two things seem like they shouldn t add up but they absolutely do they re both just somebody refusing to be finished like the song is never actually resolved it s always reaching for something else

my grandmother had a record player that sat on top of the refrigerator and i remember standing on a step stool just to watch the needle i thought something was alive in there i still sort of think that

my grandmother used to say i was singing before i was talking and honestly i believe her because i still find words a lot harder than notes like ask me to explain something and i ll probably just start humming it at you
my manager at the time told me nobody wanted a three minute guitar solo in the middle of a pop song so naturally that s the version i turned in
my mom heard wap for the first time on the radio in her car she called me and she was like belcalis just my name that s it just said my name and hung up
my mother used to say that if you can make a chord on a ukulele you can make one on anything she was wrong about a lot of things but she wasn t wrong about that
my mother used to say the church is just a stage with better acoustics  i think about that every single time i step in front of a microphone
my mum used to clean houses on the weekends so i could have guitar lessons i think about that every single time i walk onstage not in a heavy way just i know what the ticket cost

people act like auto tune ruined singing but honestly it just revealed who was already faking it the technology didn t change the problem it just turned the lights on

people always ask me what key i write in  like there s some magic answer  i wrote  gold digger  on a tuesday afternoon eating leftover chinese food  the key was that the food was cold and i was annoyed about it

people always want to know what the creative process looks like and i m like   it looks like me eating cereal at two in the morning staring at a wall  that s it  that s the whole thing  and then eventually something happens and you don t even know why
people ask me how i stay humble and i m like have you ever had a label tell you your best record isn t commercial enough that ll do it that ll absolutely do it for you

people ask me how i write so fast and i genuinely don t have an answer that sounds sane sometimes i ll wake up at like two in the morning and there s already a chorus in my head fully formed and if i don t get it down in the next ten minutes it s just gone it doesn t come from me sitting down and deciding to write it s more like catching something before it flies away

people ask why i still tour and i genuinely don t have a clean answer some of it s the obvious stuff but some of it is that i still get nervous before i walk out and i ve decided that s the part i don t ever want to fix

people hear electronic music and they think nobody s really doing anything like it s all automatic i m making decisions every four bars that a guitarist makes once a song it s just that my instrument looks like a laptop so somehow that doesn t count
people hear the accent and they think country and that s fine i am country but i also grew up listening to led zeppelin in my daddy s truck and nobody ever wants to put that in the bio

people keep asking if i m worried about the album leaking and honestly yeah a little but mostly i m terrified someone s gonna hear the demo vocals i left on track seven by accident and think that s intentional

people think being from barbados is this postcard thing like i stepped off a beach straight into a recording studio growing up there music was so tied to like shame and pride at the same time you d hear something and it d make your body move before your brain had any opinion about it at all

quincy always said the arrangement is the second song meaning you could write something beautiful and then just completely murder it with the wrong strings i think about that every single time i sit down to produce somebody else s record

quincy called me maybe three days after the session and just said that low note you sang on the bridge don t ever let anyone take that away from you i didn t even know which note he meant i still don t really but i ve thought about it probably every week since

quincy called me once this was maybe two in the afternoon his time and he just started the conversation in the middle of a thought no hello nothing he said the bridge needs one more voice a low voice something that sounds like it s already tired and i knew exactly what he meant that s not a thing you can explain to someone you either hear it or you don t
